title: Early-life adversity amplifies genetic susceptibility to major depressive disorder through stress-sensitive pathways
question: How does early-life adversity interact with genetic risk to increase population-level depression burden?
background: Major depressive disorder (MDD) exhibits substantial heritability and environmental influence. Early-life adversity (ELA)—including maltreatment, neglect, and household dysfunction—is associated with elevated MDD incidence in adolescence and adulthood. GWAS have identified hundreds of genetic variants contributing to MDD risk, but effect sizes are small. Gene-environment interaction (GxE) hypotheses propose that stress-sensitive pathways moderate the expression of genetic liability. This brief reviews mechanistic evidence linking ELA to enhanced penetrance of depression-associated loci through hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis dysregulation and inflammatory priming.
evidence_summary: GWAS of MDD implicate genes in stress-response (CRH, FKBP5) and neuroplasticity (BDNF, SLC6A4)
Meta-analyses report ELA x polygenic risk interactions for depression outcomes
Epigenetic modifications (e.g., FKBP5 methylation) associated with ELA and depression
HPA axis hyperactivity and inflammation proposed as shared mechanistic pathways
mechanistic_chain: Early-life adversity induces lasting changes in HPA axis reactivity and glucocorticoid receptor signaling
Dysregulated stress response primes inflammatory pathways (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α) in susceptible tissues
Genetic variants in stress-pathway genes alter receptor sensitivity and feedback loops
Under chronic or recurrent stress, amplified stress signaling impairs neuroplasticity and mood regulation
Population-level depression burden reflects joint distribution of genetic liability and ELA exposure
tissue_specificity: Key tissues include brain (prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, amygdala), where stress and neuroplasticity pathways converge. Peripheral biomarkers (cortisol, inflammatory markers) may proxy central processes but do not fully capture tissue-specific gene expression.
counterarguments: Many GxE studies suffer from measurement error in exposure assessment and p-hacking in candidate gene designs
Causal direction remains unclear; genetic liability may influence exposure to adversity (gene-environment correlation)
validation_criteria: Replication in large, well-powered cohorts with prospective ELA assessment
Multi-ancestry validation to assess transferability of interaction effects
Experimental manipulation of stress pathways in model systems to test causality
